clues: This name’s meaning is a single, powerful abstract concept associated with triumph.
It was used as both a personal name and a divine epithet in the ancient Roman world.
A monarch who ruled the largest empire in history and defined an entire era bore this name.
The Latin root is a noun formed from the verb ‘vincere’ — meaning ‘to conquer’.
The era named after its most famous bearer lasted from 1837 to 1901.
This Latin name simply means ‘victory’, from ‘vincere’ (to conquer).
origin: Latin, meaning ‘victory’, from the verb vincere (‘to conquer’). In Roman mythology, Victoria was the goddess of victory, equivalent to the Greek Nike. The name’s most famous bearer is Queen Victoria (1819–1901), whose 63-year reign defined the Victorian era.

clues: This name is built from a trilateral Arabic root relating to praise and commendation.
It is consistently ranked as the most common male given name on Earth.
The most famous bearer founded a major world religion in the 7th century CE.
The Arabic root ‘Ḥ-M-D’ generates words related to being praiseworthy.
Over 150 million people alive today share this name in various transliterations.
This Arabic name means ‘the praised one’, from the root Ḥ-M-D.
origin: Arabic, from the root Ḥ-M-D meaning ‘to praise’. Literally translates to ‘the praised one’ or ‘praiseworthy’. Borne by the Prophet Muhammad (c. 570–632 CE), the founder of Islam. It is the most popular male given name in the world.

clues: This name ultimately traces back to an ancient word for a precious organic gem found in the sea.
It arrived in English via Greek and Latin but may have its deepest root in a Persian word.
A medieval queen of Scotland who became a saint popularised this name in the British Isles.
The Greek ‘margaritēs’ referred to something luminous harvested from oysters.
Famous bearers include a British prime minister and the sister of Queen Elizabeth II.
This name derives from ‘margaritēs’, the Greek word for ‘pearl’.
origin: From Greek margaritēs (‘pearl’), possibly of Persian origin from marvariād. Saint Margaret of Scotland (c. 1045–1093) popularised it in Britain. The name has been borne by queens, saints, and political leaders including Margaret Thatcher.

clues: This name fuses two Old Germanic concepts — one relating to determination, the other to defence.
A conqueror who crossed the English Channel in 1066 brought this name to lasting prominence in England.
The first component, ‘wil’, relates to desire or resolve in Old High German.
Four kings of England and a famous playwright have borne this name.
The second element ‘helm’ means ‘helmet’ or ‘protection’ in Germanic languages.
This name combines ‘wil’ (will/desire) and ‘helm’ (helmet/protection), meaning ‘resolute protector’.
origin: Germanic, from Willahelm. Combines wil (‘will/desire’) and helm (‘helmet/protection’), meaning ‘resolute protector’. Introduced to England by William the Conqueror in 1066. One of the most enduring names in the English-speaking world.

clues: This name is a single Greek noun that became one of the central concepts in Western philosophy.
A magnificent 6th-century cathedral in Istanbul was dedicated to this concept.
The word also forms the second half of a term meaning ‘love of knowledge’.
It became fashionable as a given name across European royal families in the 17th century.
The famous Hagia _____ in Istanbul literally means ‘Holy Wisdom’.
This Greek name means ‘wisdom’, from the word σοφία (sophía).
origin: Greek, from σοφία (sophía), meaning ‘wisdom’. The same root gives us ‘philosophy’ (‘love of wisdom’). The Hagia Sophia in Istanbul is dedicated to this concept. The name spread through European royalty and has been one of the most popular girls’ names worldwide since the 2000s.

clues: This name derives from a Hebrew word associated with affection and emotional bonds.
The most famous bearer was a shepherd boy who defeated a giant and became a king.
It has been popular in Wales, Scotland, and Israel for centuries, each claiming a patron saint or hero.
The Hebrew root ‘d-w-d’ relates to being cherished or held dear.
Michelangelo’s most famous sculpture depicts this biblical king as a young man.
This Hebrew name means ‘beloved’, from the root d-w-d.
origin: Hebrew, from Dāwīd, meaning ‘beloved’. The most famous bearer is King David of Israel, who slew Goliath and united the Israelite tribes. The name is shared by patron saints of Wales and Scotland and remains popular across cultures.

clues: This name’s etymology is debated — it may relate to purity, torture, or even a goddess.
Early Christians linked it to the Greek word ‘katharos’ meaning ‘pure’, though this may be folk etymology.
A 4th-century martyr of Alexandria, tortured on a spiked wheel, made this name iconic.
Three of Henry VIII’s six wives bore versions of this name.
Two empresses of Russia — both known as ‘the Great’ — carried this name.
This name is associated with the Greek ‘katharos’ (pure), giving us the name Catherine.
origin: Likely from Greek Aikaterine, of debated origin. Often linked to katharos (‘pure’), though this may be a later folk etymology. Made famous by Saint Catherine of Alexandria, a 4th-century martyr. Borne by empresses, queens, and three of Henry VIII’s wives.

clues: This name evolved through a remarkable chain of languages — Hebrew to Greek to Latin to Old French to English.
Its original Hebrew form is shared with another major biblical figure who wrestled an angel.
Two of the twelve apostles in the New Testament bore this name.
Six Scottish kings and two English kings carried this name.
The original Hebrew form is Ya’aqov, which also gives us the name Jacob.
This name is the English form of Ya’aqov (Jacob), via Latin Iacomus — giving us James.
origin: From Hebrew Ya’aqov (Jacob), via Greek Iakobos and Late Latin Iacomus. The meaning is debated — possibly ‘supplanter’ or ‘may God protect’. Two apostles bore this name, and it was carried by seven Scottish and two English kings.

clues: This name exists independently in at least three unrelated language families, each with a positive meaning.
In one West African language, it carries a meaning related to divine favour and blessing.
In an ancient South Asian language, it means ‘immortal’ or ‘eternal’.
In Arabic, a related form connects to the concept of a long, flourishing life.
The Igbo meaning — ‘grace’ or ‘mercy’ — has made it popular across the African diaspora.
This name means ‘grace’ in Igbo, ‘immortal’ in Sanskrit, and ‘eternal’ in Arabic — it’s Amara.
origin: A cross-cultural name with independent roots in multiple languages. In Igbo (Nigeria), Amara means ‘grace’ or ‘mercy’. In Sanskrit, it means ‘immortal’ or ‘undying’. In Arabic, a related form means ‘eternal life’. Its multicultural appeal has made it increasingly popular worldwide.

clues: This name’s Greek roots connect to the land and the labour of cultivating it.
A legendary dragon-slaying saint made this name a symbol of chivalry across Europe.
Six British kings in succession bore this name during the 18th and 19th centuries.
The Greek word ‘geōrgos’ combines earth and work.
The patron saint of England, who allegedly slew a dragon, bears this name.
This Greek name means ‘farmer’ or ‘earth-worker’, from ‘geōrgos’ — ‘ge’ (earth) + ‘ergon’ (work).
origin: Greek, from γεωργός (geōrgos), meaning ‘farmer’ or ‘earth-worker’. Combines gē (‘earth’) and ergon (‘work’). Famous through Saint George, the dragon-slaying patron saint of England. Six British kings bore this name.

clues: This name’s meaning changes entirely depending on which kanji characters are chosen to write it.
It can mean something cold and seasonal, or something relating to joy and good fortune.
In one common writing, it refers to frozen precipitation that blankets Japan each winter.
It is a unisex name in its country of origin, popular for both boys and girls.
Written as 雪, it means ‘snow’; written as 幸, it means ‘happiness’.
This Japanese name is Yuki — most commonly meaning ‘snow’ (雪) or ‘happiness’ (幸).
origin: Japanese, with meaning determined by kanji. The most common writings are 雪 (‘snow’) and 幸 (‘happiness/fortune’). Other kanji readings include ‘gentleness’ and ‘reason’. It is a unisex name popular throughout Japan.

clues: This name’s Latin root relates to lineage, nobility, and the ruling class of ancient Rome.
The most famous bearer is a 5th-century missionary who became the patron saint of an island nation.
The Latin word ‘patricius’ originally described a member of Rome’s aristocratic elite.
Every March 17th, millions worldwide celebrate a holiday honouring this saint.
He is credited with driving all the snakes out of Ireland — though it’s almost certainly a myth.
This Latin name means ‘nobleman’, from ‘patricius’ — the patron saint Patrick of Ireland.
origin: Latin, from patricius, meaning ‘nobleman’ or ‘of noble birth’. Related to pater (‘father’). Most famously borne by Saint Patrick (c. 385–461), the Romano-British missionary who became the patron saint of Ireland. His feast day on March 17th is celebrated worldwide.

clues: This name may share its deepest root with the Latin word for ‘divine’ or ‘heavenly’.
In Roman mythology, the bearer of this name was associated with the moon, hunting, and wild forests.
Her Greek equivalent was a twin sister of Apollo.
The root ‘dius’ (divine/heavenly) connects to the same Indo-European root as ‘Zeus’ and ‘deus’.
A beloved 20th-century British princess brought this ancient name back into the modern spotlight.
This name belongs to the Roman goddess of the hunt and the moon — Diana.
origin: Latin, possibly from an Indo-European root meaning ‘divine’ or ‘heavenly’, related to dius and deus. Diana was the Roman goddess of the hunt, the moon, and nature, equivalent to the Greek Artemis. In modern times, the name is associated with Diana, Princess of Wales (1961–1997).

clues: This name comes from an Arabic root connected to flourishing, long life, and prosperity.
The second caliph of Islam, who oversaw a vast expansion of the Muslim world, bore this name.
The Arabic root ‘ʿ-M-R’ carries meanings of building, populating, and living long.
A celebrated Persian poet and mathematician — famous for his Rubáiyát — shares this name.
The name literally suggests a long and prosperous life in Arabic.
This Arabic name means ‘flourishing’ or ‘long-lived’ — Omar, from the root ʿ-M-R.
origin: Arabic, from the root ʿ-M-R meaning ‘to live long’ or ‘to flourish’. Borne by Umar ibn al-Khattab (c. 584–644), the second Rashidun caliph, and Omar Khayyám (1048–1131), the Persian poet and mathematician. One of the most common names in the Arabic-speaking world.

clues: This name’s Hebrew original is a compound involving the divine and a solemn promise.
It has generated more diminutives than almost any other English name — at least a dozen common ones.
Two queens of England, spanning from the 16th to the 21st century, bore this name.
The Hebrew components are ‘El’ (God) and a word relating to oaths or covenants.
Liz, Beth, Eliza, Betsy, Bessie, Libby, Lisa — all derive from this single name.
This Hebrew name means ‘my God is an oath’ — Elisheva in Hebrew, Elizabeth in English.
origin: From Hebrew Elisheva, combining El (‘God’) and shevua (‘oath’), meaning ‘my God is an oath’ or ‘pledged to God’. Borne by two queens of England. It has produced more diminutives than perhaps any other name: Liz, Beth, Eliza, Betsy, Bessie, Libby, Lisa, and many more.

clues: This name’s meaning depends on kanji, but the most common writings relate to expansiveness and generosity.
It was one of the most popular male names in Japan during the mid-20th century.
Common kanji choices include 博 (‘broad/extensive’) and 寛 (‘generous/tolerant’).
A famous Japanese film director of samurai epics and Shakespeare adaptations shares this name.
The director of ‘Seven Samurai’ and ‘Rashomon’ was named Akira, but his contemporary who directed ‘Harakiri’ bore this name.
This Japanese name most commonly means ‘generous’ or ‘broad’ — Hiroshi.
origin: Japanese, with meaning determined by kanji. Common writings include 博 (‘broad/generous’), 寛 (‘tolerant’), and 浩 (‘vast’). It was among the most popular Japanese male names in the Shōwa era (1926–1989). A classic, dignified name associated with generosity and broad-mindedness.

clues: This name is a medieval Spanish and Italian evolution of a much older Hebrew name about divine promises.
A powerful 15th-century queen who funded a world-changing voyage bore this name.
It shares its ultimate root with one of the most common English names, though they sound quite different.
The queen who united Spain with her husband Ferdinand and sponsored a certain Genoese explorer.
It is essentially the same name as its English cousin, which has spawned dozens of nicknames like Liz and Beth.
This is the Spanish/Italian form of Elizabeth — Isabella, meaning ‘pledged to God’.
origin: Medieval Spanish and Italian form of Elizabeth, from Hebrew Elisheva (‘my God is an oath’). The name evolved through Provençal forms. Most famously borne by Isabella I of Castile (1451–1504), who sponsored Columbus’s voyage and unified Spain with Ferdinand II of Aragon.

clues: This name’s true origin is one of the great unsolved puzzles of onomastics — no theory is universally accepted.
One theory connects it to a Celtic word for ‘bear’, another to a Roman family name.
The most legendary bearer pulled a sword from a stone and ruled from a castle called Camelot.
Possible roots include Celtic ‘artos’ (bear), Latin ‘Artorius’, or even a connection to a star name.
Knights, a round table, the quest for a holy grail — all part of this name’s legendary lore.
This name belongs to the legendary king of Camelot — Arthur, possibly from Celtic ‘artos’ (bear).
origin: Of uncertain origin — possibly Celtic from artos (‘bear’), Latin from the Roman gens name Artorius, or related to other roots. Made immortal by the legends of King Arthur and the Knights of the Round Table. The name has been borne by kings, presidents, and countless literary characters.

clues: This name comes from an Arabic root relating to weaning, abstinence, or separation.
The most famous bearer was the daughter of the Prophet of Islam, revered across the Muslim world.
A town in Portugal where a famous Marian apparition occurred in 1917 also bears this name.
She married Ali ibn Abi Talib and is considered the mother of the Prophet’s lineage.
All Sayyid and Sharif descendants of the Prophet trace their ancestry through her.
This Arabic name meaning ‘one who abstains’ belongs to the Prophet’s daughter — Fatima.
origin: Arabic, from the root F-T-M meaning ‘to wean’ or ‘to abstain’. Most famously borne by Fāṭima al-Zahrāʾ (c. 605–632), the daughter of the Prophet Muhammad and wife of Ali ibn Abi Talib. She is revered in both Sunni and Shia Islam. The Portuguese town of Fátima, site of a 1917 Marian apparition, was likely named after a Moorish princess.

clues: This name combines two Old Germanic elements — one about a dwelling, the other about authority.
Eight English kings bore this name, including one notorious for his six marriages.
The Germanic components are ‘heim’ (home) and ‘rīc’ (ruler/power).
In France, it became Henri; in Germany, Heinrich; in Italy, Enrico — all the same name.
The most infamous English bearer broke with Rome, dissolved the monasteries, and married six times.
This Germanic name means ‘ruler of the home’ — from ‘heim’ + ‘rīc’ — Henry.
origin: Germanic, from Heimrich. Combines heim (‘home’) and rīc (‘ruler/power’), meaning ‘ruler of the home’. One of the most royal names in European history, borne by eight English kings, four French kings, and seven German emperors.

clues: This name is a single Sanskrit word that has survived unchanged for thousands of years.
It appears frequently in ancient Indian texts, the Vedas and the Mahabharata, as both a name and a descriptor.
The concept it expresses is one of the most fundamental human emotions — affection and deep fondness.
In many modern Indian languages, the word is still used in everyday speech as an adjective.
It is commonly paired with other elements to form compound names popular across South Asia.
This Sanskrit name simply means ‘beloved’ or ‘dear one’ — Priya.
origin: Sanskrit, meaning ‘beloved’, ‘dear’, or ‘cherished’. One of the oldest continuously used names in South Asia, appearing in Vedic texts. It remains popular across India, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and the South Asian diaspora, both as a standalone name and as part of compounds.

clues: This name combines two Greek concepts — one about triumph, the other about a community.
A 4th-century bishop known for secret gift-giving inspired a beloved global tradition.
The first element ‘nikē’ means ‘victory’ in Greek.
This saint’s legend evolved over centuries into a red-suited figure who delivers presents.
He is the patron saint of children, sailors, and the inspiration for Santa Claus.
This Greek name means ‘victory of the people’ — from ‘nikē’ + ‘laos’ — Nicholas.
origin: Greek, from Νικόλαος (Nikólaos). Combines nikē (‘victory’) and laos (‘people’), meaning ‘victory of the people’. The legend of Saint Nicholas of Myra (270–343), known for his generosity, evolved into the Santa Claus tradition.

clues: This name comes from an Arabic root relating to being alive, vivacious, and full of life.
The most famous bearer was the youngest wife of the Prophet of Islam, known for her scholarship.
The root ‘ʿ-Y-Sh’ is connected to the concept of living well and prosperously.
She transmitted thousands of hadith and was one of the most influential women in early Islamic history.
The name literally means ‘she who lives’ or ‘alive and well’ in Arabic.
This Arabic name means ‘alive’ or ‘living’ — Aisha, from the root ʿ-Y-Sh.
origin: Arabic, from the root ʿ-Y-Sh meaning ‘to live’. Means ‘alive’, ‘living’, or ‘she who lives’. Most famously borne by Aisha bint Abi Bakr (c. 613–678), wife of the Prophet Muhammad, renowned for her intellect and scholarship. One of the most popular names in the Muslim world.

clues: This name’s two Germanic elements relate to reputation and luminosity.
Three Scottish kings bore this name, the most famous of whom won independence at Bannockburn.
The first element ‘hrōþ’ means ‘fame’ or ‘renown’ in Old Germanic.
Its short forms — Rob, Bob, Bobby, Bert — are names in their own right.
The Bruce, who defeated the English at Bannockburn in 1314, bore this name.
This Germanic name means ‘bright fame’ — from ‘hrōþ’ (fame) + ‘berht’ (bright) — Robert.
origin: Germanic, from Hrodebert. Combines hrōþ (‘fame/renown’) and berht (‘bright/illustrious’), meaning ‘bright fame’. One of the most popular names in medieval Europe. Borne by Robert the Bruce, king of Scotland, who secured Scottish independence at the Battle of Bannockburn (1314).

clues: This name is also a common Japanese word for something that blooms spectacularly each spring.
In Japanese culture, what it represents is a powerful symbol of impermanence and the beauty of mortality.
Millions of people gather each year for ‘hanami’ — the tradition of viewing what this name describes.
It is one of the most popular girls’ names in modern Japan, evoking grace and natural beauty.
The blossoms it refers to are synonymous with Japan itself — appearing on coins, art, and clothing.
This Japanese name means ‘cherry blossom’ — Sakura (桜).
origin: Japanese, meaning ‘cherry blossom’, written as 桜. Cherry blossoms are Japan’s unofficial national flower and a profound cultural symbol of the transience of life (mono no aware). The name has been popular for girls throughout Japanese history and remains one of the top names in modern Japan.

clues: This name is one of the few Anglo-Saxon names to survive the Norman Conquest intact.
Its two Old English components relate to wealth and guardianship.
Three medieval Anglo-Saxon kings bore this name, including one who was later made a saint.
The first element ‘ēad’ means ‘riches’ or ‘prosperity’ in Old English.
A 20th-century British king abdicated the throne to marry an American divorcee — he bore this name.
This Old English name means ‘wealthy guardian’ — from ‘ēad’ (wealth) + ‘weard’ (guardian) — Edward.
origin: Old English, from Ēadweard. Combines ēad (‘wealth/fortune’) and weard (‘guardian’), meaning ‘wealthy guardian’. Survived the Norman Conquest due to the popularity of Saint Edward the Confessor. Eight English kings have borne this name, including Edward VIII who abdicated in 1936.

clues: This name derives from a Slavic word that expresses one of humanity’s most sustaining emotions.
It became internationally known through a perfect-scoring gymnast at the 1976 Olympics.
The root word ‘nadezhda’ is one of three theological virtues in Russian Orthodox tradition.
The name is popular across Russia, Eastern Europe, the Arab world, and francophone Africa.
The gymnast who scored the first perfect 10 in Olympic history bore this name.
This Slavic name means ‘hope’ — Nadia, from Russian ‘nadezhda’.
origin: Slavic, a diminutive of Nadezhda, meaning ‘hope’. The name spread widely through Russian and Eastern European culture, as well as into the Arabic-speaking world. Made internationally famous by Nadia Comăneci, the Romanian gymnast who scored the first perfect 10 at the 1976 Montreal Olympics.

clues: This name’s two Greek components express a deep affinity for a specific animal.
The father of Alexander the Great bore this name and gave it to a Macedonian dynasty.
The first element ‘philos’ means ‘loving’ or ‘fond of’ in Greek.
Six kings of France and five kings of Spain have borne this name.
The second element ‘hippos’ refers to an animal crucial to ancient warfare and transport.
This Greek name means ‘lover of horses’ — from ‘philos’ + ‘hippos’ — Philip.
origin: Greek, from Φίλιππος (Phílippos). Combines philos (‘loving’) and hippos (‘horse’), meaning ‘lover of horses’. Borne by Philip II of Macedon, father of Alexander the Great, and by numerous European kings. The Duke of Edinburgh also bore this name.

clues: This name comes from an Arabic word describing the darkness that falls after sunset.
The most famous bearer is a tragic heroine in one of the great love stories of Persian literature.
The Arabic root ‘L-Y-L’ relates to the hours between dusk and dawn.
The poet Nizami wrote an epic about her doomed love affair with Majnun in the 12th century.
The love story of Majnun and _____ is often called the Romeo and Juliet of the East.
This Arabic/Persian name means ‘night’ — Leila, from the root L-Y-L.
origin: Arabic, from layl meaning ‘night’. The name gained romantic associations through the tragic love story of Layla and Majnun, immortalised by the Persian poet Nizami Ganjavi (1141–1209). The tale is considered one of the greatest love stories in world literature.

clues: This name derives from a Germanic word associated with ordinary men — the common folk.
A Frankish emperor crowned in 800 CE transformed this name into a synonym for kingship itself.
The Germanic root ‘karl’ originally meant ‘free man’ of the lowest rank.
In many languages, the very word for ‘king’ derives from this name: ‘könig’, ‘kralj’, ‘král’.
The most recent king of the United Kingdom, crowned in 2023, bears this name.
This Germanic name means ‘free man’ — from ‘karl’ — Charles, as in Charlemagne (‘Carolus Magnus’).
origin: Germanic, from karl meaning ‘free man’. The name’s prestige comes from Charlemagne (Carolus Magnus, c. 748–814), whose name became synonymous with kingship. Several Slavic and Scandinavian words for ‘king’ derive from it. Borne by numerous European kings, most recently King Charles III.

clues: This name’s Sanskrit meaning relates to agriculture — specifically, a line drawn through the earth.
The most famous bearer is the heroine of one of the two great epic poems of ancient India.
Her name literally means ‘furrow’, and legend says she was born from a ploughed field.
She was abducted by the demon king Ravana, setting in motion a legendary war.
Her husband, an avatar of Vishnu, waged war across an ocean to rescue her.
This Sanskrit name means ‘furrow’ — Sita, the heroine of the Ramayana.
origin: Sanskrit, meaning ‘furrow’, referring to a line ploughed in the earth. In Hindu mythology, Sita is the heroine of the Ramayana, wife of Rama. Legend says she was discovered in a furrow by King Janaka. She is revered as an ideal of feminine virtue across South and Southeast Asia.

clues: This name derives from a single Greek adjective describing a quintessentially masculine quality.
One of the first two apostles called by Jesus in the New Testament bore this name.
The Greek root ‘andr-’ appears in many English words relating to males.
He is the patron saint of Scotland, Russia, Greece, and Romania simultaneously.
The white diagonal cross associated with Scotland’s flag is named after this saint.
This Greek name means ‘manly’ or ‘courageous’ — from ‘andreios’ — Andrew.
origin: Greek, from Ἀνδρέας (Andréas), derived from anēr (‘man’) via andreios (‘manly/courageous’). Saint Andrew was one of the first apostles and is the patron saint of Scotland, Russia, and several other nations. The Scottish flag (St Andrew’s Cross) is named in his honour.

clues: This character-based name can carry very different meanings depending on which Chinese character is chosen.
Common meanings include ‘beautiful’, ‘plum’, or ‘every/each’ — all from different characters.
Written as 美, it appears in the Chinese name for ‘America’ (美国, literally ‘beautiful country’).
Written as 梅, it refers to the plum blossom — China’s national flower.
It is one of the most common elements in Chinese feminine names.
This Chinese name is Mei — meaning ‘beautiful’ (美) or ‘plum blossom’ (梅).
origin: Chinese, with meaning determined by character. The most common are 美 (měi, ‘beautiful’) and 梅 (méi, ‘plum blossom’). The plum blossom is the national flower of the Republic of China. Mei is one of the most versatile and popular elements in Chinese naming.

clues: This name is a Hebrew compound in which the divine acts as a judge or arbiter.
The most famous biblical bearer survived a night in a den of dangerous predators.
The first element ‘Dan’ relates to judgement and law in Hebrew.
The Old Testament book bearing this name contains famous prophecies and the story of the fiery furnace.
He was thrown into a lions’ den by King Darius but emerged unharmed.
This Hebrew name means ‘God is my judge’ — from ‘Dan’ (judge) + ‘El’ (God) — Daniel.
origin: Hebrew, combining Dan (‘judge’) and El (‘God’), meaning ‘God is my judge’. The biblical Daniel was a prophet known for surviving the lions’ den and interpreting dreams. The name has been consistently popular across the Christian and Jewish worlds for centuries.

clues: This name combines two elements from Norse mythology and language — one divine, one beautiful.
It was popularised across Scandinavia by medieval queens and remains a classic Nordic name.
The first element refers to ‘Ing’, an alternative name for the Norse fertility god Freyr.
A Swedish-born Hollywood actress, famous for ‘Casablanca’, carried this name to global fame.
The second element ‘fríðr’ means ‘beautiful’ or ‘beloved’ in Old Norse.
This Old Norse name means ‘Ing’s beauty’ — from ‘Ing’ (a god) + ‘fríðr’ (beautiful) — Ingrid.
origin: Old Norse, combining Ing (a name for the god Freyr) and fríðr (‘beautiful/beloved’), meaning ‘Ing’s beauty’ or ‘beautiful through Ing’. Popular among Scandinavian royalty. Made internationally famous by actress Ingrid Bergman (1915–1982).

clues: This name’s Hebrew meaning involves a relationship between divine attention and mortal need.
The most famous bearer was the last of the biblical judges, who anointed the first two kings of Israel.
Some scholars translate it as ‘heard by God’, others as ‘name of God’.
Two books of the Old Testament bear this name.
He anointed both Saul and a young shepherd who would become Israel’s greatest king.
This Hebrew name means ‘God has heard’ or ‘name of God’ — Samuel, from ‘Shemu’el’.
origin: Hebrew, from Shemu’el, variously interpreted as ‘God has heard’ (from shama, ‘to hear’) or ‘name of God’. The biblical Samuel was the last judge of Israel and the prophet who anointed kings Saul and David. Two books of the Old Testament bear his name.

clues: This name is a Greek word that originally described new plant growth in early spring.
In Greek mythology, it was an epithet of Demeter, the goddess of agriculture and harvest.
The word refers to the first pale green shoots that emerge from the soil after winter.
It appears in a famous ancient Greek pastoral romance alongside the name Daphnis.
Demeter was called by this epithet in her role as protector of young crops.
This Greek name means ‘young green shoot’ or ‘blooming’ — Chloe, from χλόη (khloé).
origin: Greek, from χλόη (khloé), meaning ‘young green shoot’ or ‘blooming’. It was an epithet of Demeter, goddess of the harvest, in her role nurturing young crops. Also appears in the ancient pastoral romance ‘Daphnis and Chloe’ by Longus (2nd century CE).

clues: This name belongs to a West African naming system based on the day of the week a child is born.
In the Akan tradition of Ghana, every day of the week has a corresponding male and female name.
A Ghanaian diplomat who led the United Nations from 1997 to 2006 bore this name.
He was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2001 for his work on global cooperation.
The day this name corresponds to is the fifth day of the traditional Akan week.
This Akan name is given to boys born on Friday — Kofi, as in former UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan.
origin: Akan (Ghana), a day name given to males born on Friday. Part of the traditional Akan naming system where each day of the week has a male and female name. Made internationally famous by Kofi Annan (1938–2018), the Ghanaian diplomat who served as the seventh UN Secretary-General and won the Nobel Peace Prize.

clues: This name is a Hebrew word for a specific domestic farm animal.
In the Book of Genesis, the bearer of this name was Jacob’s favoured wife, for whom he laboured fourteen years.
The Hebrew word refers to a female sheep — symbolising gentleness and pastoral beauty.
Jacob worked seven years for her father Laban, was tricked into marrying her sister Leah, then worked seven more.
The animal this name describes has long been a biblical metaphor for innocence and meekness.
This Hebrew name means ‘ewe’ (a female sheep) — Rachel, from the Hebrew rāḥēl.
origin: Hebrew, from rāḥēl meaning ‘ewe’ (a female sheep). In the Bible, Rachel was the beloved wife of Jacob, for whom he laboured fourteen years. She was the mother of Joseph and Benjamin. The name symbolises beauty, gentleness, and devotion.

clues: This name is the Slavic evolution of a Hebrew name that passed through Greek and Latin.
Several tsars of Russia bore this name, including one nicknamed ‘the Terrible’.
Its ultimate root is a Hebrew name meaning ‘God is gracious’.
The journey from Hebrew Yohanan to this Slavic form crossed four languages.
The English equivalent of this name is John, the French is Jean, the Spanish is Juan.
This is the Russian/Slavic form of John — Ivan, ultimately from Hebrew Yohanan.
origin: Slavic form of the Hebrew name Yohanan (‘God is gracious’), via Greek Ioannes and Latin Iohannes. Borne by six Russian tsars, most notoriously Ivan IV ‘the Terrible’ (1530–1584). It is one of the most quintessentially Russian names and has equivalents in nearly every European language.

clues: This name is a French shortening of a longer Germanic name about nobility and character.
The longer original form combines Old German words for ‘noble’ and ‘kind/type’.
A 19th-century English author sent this name’s bearer down a rabbit hole into a fantastical world.
The original form ‘Adalheidis’ is also the source of the names Adelaide and Heidi.
Lewis Carroll’s most famous character follows a white rabbit and encounters a grinning cat.
This name is a French form of the Germanic Adalheidis — meaning ‘noble kind’ — Alice.
origin: From Old French Aalis, a short form of Germanic Adalheidis. Combines adal (‘noble’) and heid (‘kind/type’), meaning ‘of noble kind’. The same root gives us Adelaide and Heidi. Made immortal by Lewis Carroll’s ‘Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland’ (1865).

clues: This Arabic name describes the deepest possible form of friendship — beyond ordinary companionship.
In Islamic tradition, this title was bestowed upon the Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham) by God.
The root ‘Kh-L-L’ conveys intimacy and a bond that permeates the soul.
A Lebanese-American poet and philosopher who wrote ‘The Prophet’ bore this name.
The Arabic title meaning ‘Friend of God’ uses this exact word and refers to Abraham.
This Arabic name means ‘close friend’ or ‘intimate companion’ — Khalil, from the root Kh-L-L.
origin: Arabic, from the root Kh-L-L meaning ‘to befriend intimately’. Means ‘close friend’ or ‘intimate companion’. In Islam, Ibrahim (Abraham) holds the title Khalilullah (‘Friend of God’). Made famous in the West by Khalil Gibran (1883–1931), the Lebanese-American author of ‘The Prophet’.

clues: This name may derive from a Greek word meaning ‘torch’, ‘light’, or ‘shining’.
The most famous bearer was said to be the most beautiful woman in the ancient world.
Her abduction — or elopement — to Troy triggered a ten-year war.
The Greek word for ‘torch’ or the word for ‘moon’ (selēnē) may be the root.
She was the daughter of Zeus and Leda, and her face ‘launched a thousand ships’.
This Greek name possibly means ‘shining light’ — Helen, as in Helen of Troy.
origin: Greek, from Ἑλένη (Helénē), possibly related to helenē (‘torch’) or selēnē (‘moon’). Helen of Troy, daughter of Zeus, was considered the most beautiful woman in the Greek world. Her abduction by Paris sparked the Trojan War, inspiring Homer’s Iliad.

clues: This name is a Hebrew compound in which the divine performs an act of restoration.
In Jewish, Christian, and Islamic tradition, the bearer of this name is one of the archangels.
The first component means ‘to heal’ or ‘to mend’ in Hebrew.
A Renaissance painter, considered one of the three great masters alongside Leonardo and Michelangelo, bore this name.
The archangel associated with healing is named from the Hebrew ‘rapha’ (to heal) + ‘El’ (God).
This Hebrew name means ‘God has healed’ — Rafael, from ‘rapha’ + ‘El’.
origin: Hebrew, combining rapha (‘to heal’) and El (‘God’), meaning ‘God has healed’. Raphael is one of the archangels in Jewish, Christian, and Islamic tradition, associated with healing. The Renaissance painter Raphael Sanzio (1483–1520) is considered one of the greatest artists of all time.

clues: This name entered Slavic languages via a Viking route — its Norse original sounds quite different.
A 10th-century Kievan princess who converted to Christianity bore this name and was later made a saint.
The Norse original meant ‘holy’, ‘blessed’, or ‘prosperous’.
The original Norse form of this name is Helga — the Slavic adaptation changed the initial sound.
Saint _____ of Kyiv is one of the most important figures in the Christianisation of the Slavic world.
This is the Slavic form of Norse Helga — Olga, meaning ‘holy’ or ‘blessed’.
origin: Slavic adaptation of Old Norse Helga, meaning ‘holy’, ‘blessed’, or ‘prosperous’. Made famous by Saint Olga of Kyiv (c. 890–969), a Varangian-born princess who was one of the first rulers of Kievan Rus’ to convert to Christianity. She was canonised as the first Russian saint.

clues: This name derives from an Aramaic word describing a specific family relationship.
The most famous biblical bearer was an apostle known for his scepticism about the Resurrection.
The Aramaic word ‘t’oma’ means ‘paired’ or ‘doubled’.
The phrase ‘doubting _____’ has entered the English language as an idiom for scepticism.
A medieval philosopher-saint who wrote the ‘Summa Theologica’ also bore this name — Aquinas.
This Aramaic name means ‘twin’ — Thomas, from ‘t’oma’.
origin: Aramaic, from t’oma meaning ‘twin’. Borne by the Apostle Thomas, called ‘Didymus’ (the Greek equivalent of ‘twin’). His doubt about the Resurrection gave English the phrase ‘Doubting Thomas’. Also borne by Saint Thomas Aquinas (1225–1274), one of the most influential philosophers in Western history.

clues: This name belongs to one of the most important deities in the Norse pantheon.
She presided over love, beauty, fertility, and also had a fierce association with war and death.
Her sacred animal was the cat, and her chariot was said to be pulled by two large felines.
In Norse mythology, she received half of the battle-slain — the other half went to Odin.
A day of the English week is named after her — or possibly after the related goddess Frigg.
This is the name of the Norse goddess of love and beauty — Freya, meaning ‘lady’ or ‘noblewoman’.
origin: Old Norse, from Freyja meaning ‘lady’ or ‘noblewoman’, related to the German Frau. Freya was the Norse goddess of love, beauty, fertility, war, and death. She received half of the battle-slain in her hall Fólkvangr. The name has surged in popularity in Britain and Scandinavia in recent decades.

clues: This Hebrew name expresses a wish for divine increase or addition.
The most famous biblical bearer was sold into slavery by his brothers but rose to become viceroy of Egypt.
The root ‘yasaph’ means ‘to add’ or ‘to increase’ in Hebrew.
In the New Testament, the earthly father of Jesus also bore this name.
His coat of many colours and his ability to interpret dreams are central to his story.
This Hebrew name means ‘God will add’ or ‘he will increase’ — Joseph, from Yosef.
origin: Hebrew, from Yosef, meaning ‘God will add’ or ‘he will increase’, from the root yasaph (‘to add’). In Genesis, Joseph was Jacob’s favoured son, sold into slavery but rising to power in Egypt. In the New Testament, Joseph of Nazareth was the husband of Mary.

clues: This name has independent roots in multiple languages, all carrying bright, positive meanings.
In Arabic, it is associated with radiance, blooming, and the beauty of flowers.
In Hebrew, a related form means ‘seed’ or ‘dawn’ — the beginning of something new.
A daughter of King Charles III and a major Spanish fashion brand share this name.
The Arabic root connects to the word for ‘flower’ (zahra) and ‘radiance’.
This name means ‘flower’ or ‘radiance’ in Arabic and ‘dawn’ in Hebrew — Zara.
origin: Multiple origins: from Arabic zahra (‘flower/radiance’), Hebrew zerah (‘seed/dawn’), or a variant of Sarah. Borne by Zara Tindall (née Phillips), daughter of Princess Anne. Also the name of the global fashion retailer founded in Spain in 1975.

clues: This name may derive from Mars, the Roman god of war, making it essentially ‘belonging to Mars’.
It was one of the most common praenomina (first names) in ancient Rome.
A Roman emperor-philosopher who wrote ‘Meditations’ while campaigning on the frontier bore this name.
The connection to Mars gave this name martial, powerful connotations in the Roman world.
The stoic philosopher-emperor who ruled from 161 to 180 CE bore this name — _____ Aurelius.
This Latin name means ‘dedicated to Mars’ — Marcus, as in Marcus Aurelius.
origin: Latin, probably derived from Mars, the Roman god of war, meaning ‘dedicated to Mars’ or ‘warlike’. One of the most common Roman praenomina. Most famously borne by Marcus Aurelius (121–180 CE), the Stoic philosopher-emperor whose ‘Meditations’ remains one of the most widely read works of philosophy.

clues: This name derives from a Greek word that describes the act of rising or standing up again.
It gained deep significance in early Christian theology as a metaphor for life after death.
A Russian grand duchess, rumoured for decades to have survived her family’s execution, bore this name.
The root ‘anastasis’ is used in Greek theology to describe a central event of the Christian faith.
The legend of the lost Romanov princess inspired films, books, and Broadway musicals.
This Greek name means ‘resurrection’ — Anastasia, from ‘anastasis’.
origin: Greek, from ἀνάστασις (anástasis), meaning ‘resurrection’. Popular among early Christians for its theological significance. Most famously associated with Grand Duchess Anastasia Nikolaevna of Russia (1901–1918), whose rumoured survival inspired enduring legends.

clues: This name comes from a single Old Germanic word relating to the intellect.
In Norse mythology, one of Odin’s two ravens bore the Old Norse form of this name.
The Germanic root ‘hug’ means ‘mind’, ‘thought’, or ‘spirit’.
A towering French novelist, author of ‘Les Misérables’, carried this as his surname (and it derives from the same root).
Odin’s raven Huginn, representing ‘thought’, shares the same root as this name.
This Germanic name means ‘mind’ or ‘intellect’ — Hugo, from Old Germanic ‘hug’.
origin: Germanic, from hug meaning ‘mind’, ‘thought’, or ‘spirit’. Related to Old Norse Huginn, the name of one of Odin’s ravens representing thought. Common across European languages. Associated with Victor Hugo (1802–1885), though Hugo was his surname.

clues: This Hebrew name carries a meaning associated with royalty and high social standing.
In the Book of Genesis, God changed this woman’s name from an earlier form as a sign of a covenant.
Her original name was Sarai, meaning ‘my princess’, before it was altered to a more universal form.
She gave birth to Isaac at the age of ninety, fulfilling a divine promise.
The name means ‘princess’ in Hebrew and she is considered a matriarch of three world religions.
This Hebrew name means ‘princess’ or ‘noblewoman’ — Sarah, wife of Abraham.
origin: Hebrew, meaning ‘princess’ or ‘noblewoman’. In Genesis, God changed her name from Sarai (‘my princess’) to Sarah (‘princess’ in a universal sense) as part of His covenant with Abraham. She is considered a matriarch in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam.

clues: This name exists independently in at least five unrelated languages, each with a different meaning.
In Hawaiian, it means ‘sea’; in Japanese, depending on kanji, it can mean ‘ocean’ or ‘shell’.
In Navajo, it means ‘willow tree’; in Scandinavian languages, its origin is debated.
Its extreme brevity and universal pronounceability have made it popular across cultures.
In a famous Hans Christian Andersen story, a boy with this name is taken by the Snow Queen.
This multicultural name is Kai — meaning ‘sea’ in Hawaiian, ‘ocean’ in Japanese, and more.
origin: A name with independent roots in multiple languages. Hawaiian: ‘sea’. Japanese: ‘ocean’ or ‘shell’ (depending on kanji). Navajo: ‘willow tree’. Scandinavian: possibly from Old Norse meaning ‘earth’. Its brevity and cross-cultural appeal have made it increasingly popular worldwide.

clues: This name’s Latin root describes someone who brings a specific positive state to others.
In one of the greatest works of Italian literature, she serves as the poet’s heavenly guide through Paradise.
The Latin ‘beatus’ means ‘blessed’ or ‘happy’, and this name is built from its feminine form.
Dante Alighieri immortalised a woman with this name in the ‘Divine Comedy’.
She guides Dante through the celestial spheres of Paradise after Virgil departs.
This Latin name means ‘she who makes happy’ or ‘bringer of joy’ — Beatrice, from ‘beatus’.
origin: Latin, from beatrix meaning ‘she who makes happy’ or ‘bringer of blessings’, from beatus (‘blessed/happy’). Immortalised by Dante Alighieri in the Divine Comedy, where Beatrice guides the poet through Paradise. Also borne by several European queens and princesses.

clues: This Sanskrit name is also a common noun referring to one of the most essential celestial objects.
In Hindu mythology, the deity associated with this word rides a chariot drawn by seven horses across the sky.
The object it describes has been worshipped as a god in virtually every ancient civilisation.
In the Hindu calendar, the day named after this word corresponds to Sunday.
A legendary Indian musician who popularised the sitar in the West shared this name — _____ Shankar.
This Sanskrit name means ‘sun’ — Ravi, the Hindu solar deity.
origin: Sanskrit, meaning ‘sun’. Ravi is one of the twelve Adityas (solar deities) in Hindu mythology. The word gives its name to Ravivara (Sunday) in the Hindu calendar. Made famous in the West by sitar virtuoso Ravi Shankar (1920–2012).

clues: This name is a French feminine diminutive of a much older, shorter Germanic name.
The original masculine form means ‘free man’ in Old Germanic.
Three Brontë sisters were writers, and the eldest — author of ‘Jane Eyre’ — bore this name.
The masculine root also gave rise to Charlemagne and to the English word ‘churl’.
A popular dessert of layered sponge fingers and cream also bears this name.
This is the French feminine form of Charles — Charlotte, meaning ‘free woman’.
origin: French feminine diminutive of Charles, from Germanic karl (‘free man’). The name has been popular among European royalty and in literature. Most famously borne by Charlotte Brontë (1816–1855), author of ‘Jane Eyre’. Also the name of a British princess born in 2015.

clues: This name is the Arabic form of one of the most important patriarchal figures across three world religions.
He is revered as the ‘father of nations’ in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam.
In the Quran, he is described as a ‘hanif’ — one who turns away from idolatry toward the one God.
He is credited with building the Kaaba in Mecca alongside his son Ismail.
The Hebrew original of this name means ‘father of many’ or ‘father of multitudes’.
This is the Arabic form of Abraham — Ibrahim, meaning ‘father of many’.
origin: Arabic form of Hebrew Abraham, meaning ‘father of many’ or ‘father of multitudes’. Ibrahim is one of the most important prophets in Islam, revered for his monotheism and his role in building the Kaaba in Mecca. The name is among the most popular in the Muslim world.

clues: This name derives from a Latin word relating to brightness and illumination.
A Sicilian saint martyred during the Diocletian persecution bears this name and is the patron saint of sight.
The root ‘lux’ (light) gives English words like ‘lucid’ and ‘translucent’.
Every December 13th in Scandinavia, a festival of light celebrates this saint with candlelit processions.
She is the patron saint of the blind, and her feast day falls near the winter solstice.
This Latin name means ‘light’ — Lucia, from ‘lux’ (light).
origin: Latin, from lux meaning ‘light’. Saint Lucia of Syracuse (283–304) was martyred during the Diocletian persecution and became the patron saint of sight and the blind. Her feast day on December 13th is celebrated with candlelit processions across Scandinavia.

clues: This name’s two Germanic components describe a leader with unyielding authority.
Three English kings bore this name, including one nicknamed ‘the Lionheart’.
The first element ‘ric’ means ‘ruler’ or ‘king’ in Old Germanic.
The Lionheart spent most of his reign fighting in the Crusades and barely set foot in England.
The second element ‘hard’ means ‘brave’ or ‘strong’ — the full name suggests a powerful ruler.
This Germanic name means ‘brave ruler’ — from ‘ric’ (ruler) + ‘hard’ (brave) — Richard.
origin: Germanic, from Ricohard. Combines ric (‘ruler/king’) and hard (‘brave/strong’), meaning ‘brave ruler’. Borne by three kings of England, most famously Richard I ‘the Lionheart’ (1157–1199), a central figure of the Third Crusade.

clues: This name derives from an Arabic root relating to authority, command, and governance.
The masculine form of this name is a title used for princes and military commanders across the Arab world.
The root ‘A-M-R’ generates words relating to ordering, commanding, and leading.
The English word ‘emir’ comes from the same Arabic root.
The feminine form means ‘princess’ or ‘one who commands’.
This Arabic name means ‘princess’ or ‘commander’ — Amira, the feminine of Amir.
origin: Arabic, the feminine form of Amir, from the root A-M-R meaning ‘to command’. Means ‘princess’ or ‘one who commands’. The masculine form Amir is a title equivalent to ‘prince’ or ‘commander’, giving English the word ‘emir’.

clues: This name’s origin is disputed — it may come from Old Irish, Old English, or Old Norse.
In Irish mythology, a great warrior grandson of Fionn mac Cumhaill bore this name.
One theory combines Old Irish ‘os’ (deer) with ‘cara’ (friend/lover).
A famous Irish wit and playwright, jailed for gross indecency in 1895, bore this name.
The golden statuettes awarded each year by the Academy of Motion Picture Arts also bear this name.
This name possibly means ‘deer friend’ in Irish — Oscar, as in Oscar Wilde.
origin: Disputed origin — possibly Old Irish from os (‘deer’) and cara (‘friend’), or Old English from os (‘god’) and gar (‘spear’). In Irish mythology, Oscar was the grandson of Fionn mac Cumhaill. The name was revived by James Macpherson’s Ossianic poems. Famous bearers include Oscar Wilde (1854–1900).

clues: This Hebrew name expresses a quality of charm, delight, and agreeableness.
In the Book of Ruth, the bearer of this name loses her husband and sons but gains a famously loyal daughter-in-law.
After her losses, she asked to be called ‘Mara’ (bitter) instead, feeling the name no longer suited her.
Her daughter-in-law Ruth’s declaration of loyalty is one of the most quoted passages in the Bible.
The name means ‘pleasant’ or ‘lovely’ in Hebrew.
This Hebrew name means ‘pleasantness’ or ‘delight’ — Naomi, from the Book of Ruth.
origin: Hebrew, meaning ‘pleasant’, ‘lovely’, or ‘delightful’. In the Book of Ruth, Naomi is the mother-in-law whose story of loss and redemption, and whose relationship with Ruth, is one of the Bible’s most celebrated narratives.

clues: This Slavic name derives from the name of an ancient Greek goddess associated with agriculture and the earth.
The Greek original means ‘devoted to’ or ‘follower of’ this goddess.
The goddess in question presided over grain, harvest, and the cycle of seasons.
A famous Russian chemist who created the periodic table of elements bore this name.
The goddess’s name combines ‘de’ (possibly ‘earth’) and ‘meter’ (mother) — Demeter.
This is the Slavic form of Demetrius, meaning ‘devoted to Demeter’ — Dmitri.
origin: Slavic form of Greek Demetrius, meaning ‘devoted to Demeter’, the goddess of the harvest. Borne by several Russian tsars and by Dmitri Mendeleev (1834–1907), who created the periodic table of elements. One of the most classic Russian names.

clues: This name may derive from the Persian word for ‘star’ or from the name of a Babylonian goddess.
The bearer of this name in the Bible was a Jewish queen who saved her people from genocide in Persia.
The connection to the Babylonian goddess Ishtar is debated but widely discussed by scholars.
The Jewish holiday of Purim celebrates the events of her biblical story.
She approached King Ahasuerus (Xerxes) uninvited to plead for her people — risking death.
This name possibly means ‘star’ in Persian — Esther, the heroine of Purim.
origin: Possibly from Persian ‘setareh’ (‘star’) or related to the Babylonian goddess Ishtar. In the Bible, Esther was a Jewish queen of Persia who saved her people from destruction. Her story is celebrated annually during the Jewish festival of Purim.

clues: This name is an Irish short form of a much longer Germanic name about willpower and defence.
It has been the most popular boys’ name in the United States for several consecutive years.
The original longer form combines ‘wil’ (desire/will) and ‘helm’ (helmet/protection).
Despite being Irish in form, its etymology is entirely Germanic — brought to Ireland by the Anglo-Normans.
The full original name from which it derives is one of the most classic English royal names.
This is the Irish short form of William — Liam, meaning ‘resolute protector’.
origin: Irish short form of Uilliam, the Irish version of William, from Germanic Willahelm (‘resolute protector’). Though Irish in usage, the name’s roots are entirely Germanic. It has been the most popular boys’ name in the US since 2017.

clues: This name is the Arabic and Aramaic form of one of the most venerated names across Abrahamic religions.
She is the only woman mentioned by name in the Quran, where an entire surah is dedicated to her.
The original Hebrew meaning is debated — theories include ‘bitter sea’, ‘beloved’, or ‘rebellious’.
In Islam, she is considered the greatest woman who ever lived and gave birth to the Prophet Isa (Jesus).
Surah 19 of the Quran is named after her and tells the story of a miraculous birth.
This is the Arabic form of Mary — Maryam, mother of Isa in Islam and Jesus in Christianity.
origin: Arabic/Aramaic form of Hebrew Miriam/Mary. The only woman named in the Quran, with Surah 19 (Maryam) dedicated to her story. Meaning debated — possibly ‘beloved’, ‘bitter’, or ‘rebellious’. Revered in Islam as the mother of the Prophet Isa (Jesus) and considered the greatest of all women.

clues: This name comes from a Greek word meaning ‘to hold fast’, ‘to restrain’, or ‘to check’.
The most famous bearer was the greatest warrior of Troy, who fought to defend his city and family.
He killed Patroclus, which provoked his own nemesis to return to battle.
He was ultimately slain by Achilles and his body was dragged around the walls of Troy.
His farewell to his wife Andromache and infant son is one of the most poignant scenes in the Iliad.
This Greek name means ‘holding fast’ or ‘steadfast’ — Hector, the Trojan hero of Homer’s Iliad.
origin: Greek, from Ἕκτωρ (Héktōr), related to ekhein (‘to hold/restrain’), meaning ‘holding fast’ or ‘steadfast’. Hector was the greatest Trojan warrior in Homer’s Iliad, son of King Priam and defender of Troy. His death at the hands of Achilles is a climactic moment of the epic.

clues: This name is the Arabic form of a Hebrew name meaning ‘God will add’ or ‘he will increase’.
In the Quran, Surah 12 is devoted entirely to the beautiful story of this prophet.
He was thrown into a well by his jealous brothers but rose to become a powerful ruler in Egypt.
The Quran describes his story as ‘the most beautiful of stories’ (ahsan al-qasas).
His ability to interpret dreams brought him from prison to the court of a pharaoh.
This is the Arabic form of Joseph — Yusuf, from Hebrew Yosef meaning ‘God will add’.
origin: Arabic form of Hebrew Yosef (Joseph), meaning ‘God will add’. Surah Yusuf (Quran 12) tells his complete story, calling it ‘the most beautiful of stories’. Like the biblical Joseph, he was betrayed by his brothers, enslaved, and rose to power in Egypt through his gift of dream interpretation.

clues: This name derives from a Celtic word meaning ‘exalted’ or ‘high’.
In Irish mythology, a triple goddess of poetry, healing, and smithcraft bore the original form of this name.
Saint Brigid of Kildare, one of Ireland’s three patron saints, shares this root.
The Celtic root ‘brig’ means ‘high’, ‘noble’, or ‘exalted’.
A French film actress who became a cultural icon of the 1960s carried this name — _____ Bardot.
This Celtic name means ‘exalted one’ — Brigitte, from Celtic ‘brig’ (high/noble).
origin: French/German form of Irish Brigid, from Celtic brig meaning ‘exalted’ or ‘high’. The goddess Brigid was a triple deity of poetry, healing, and smithcraft. Saint Brigid of Kildare (c. 451–525) is one of Ireland’s patron saints. Made glamorous by actress Brigitte Bardot (b. 1934).

clues: This name derives from a Hebrew word relating to the earth and the substance from which humans were formed.
In the Abrahamic creation narrative, the bearer was the first human being.
He was placed in the Garden of Eden and is considered the first human in Abrahamic tradition.
Some scholars translate the name simply as ‘man’ or ‘human being’ in the generic sense.
He was placed in the Garden of Eden and created from the dust of the ground.
This Hebrew name means ‘man’ or ‘from the earth’ — Adam, related to ‘adamah’ (ground).
origin: Hebrew, meaning ‘man’ or ‘human being’, related to adamah (‘ground/earth’), reflecting the creation narrative in Genesis where Adam was formed from the dust of the earth. The name appears across Judaism, Christianity, and Islam as the first human being.

clues: This name derives from a Latin word expressing physical and moral robustness.
The root ‘valens’ also gives us English words like ‘valid’, ‘valour’, and ‘valiant’.
The first woman to travel to space bore this name, launching aboard Vostok 6 in 1963.
Saint Valentine, whose feast day became synonymous with romance, shares the same root.
The Soviet cosmonaut who orbited Earth 48 times was named _____ Tereshkova.
This Latin name means ‘strong’ or ‘healthy’ — Valentina, from ‘valens’ (strong).
origin: Latin, feminine of Valentinus, from valens meaning ‘strong’, ‘healthy’, or ‘vigorous’. Shares its root with Saint Valentine. Made famous by Valentina Tereshkova (b. 1937), the first woman in space, who orbited Earth aboard Vostok 6 in June 1963.

clues: This name’s most common kanji carries a meaning relating to clarity, brightness, or intelligence.
It became one of the defining names in 20th-century Japanese cinema.
Written as 明, it means ‘bright’ or ‘clear’; written as 晃, it means ‘radiant’.
The director of ‘Seven Samurai’, ‘Rashomon’, and ‘Ikiru’ bore this name — _____ Kurosawa.
A landmark 1988 anime film set in a dystopian Neo-Tokyo also uses this name as its title.
This Japanese name means ‘bright’ or ‘clear’ — Akira (明).
origin: Japanese, most commonly written as 明 (bright/clear) or 晃 (radiant). Made internationally famous by filmmaker Akira Kurosawa (1910–1998) and by the landmark anime film ‘Akira’ (1988). A unisex name, though more commonly given to males.

clues: This name may derive from a Hebrew word meaning ‘companion’, ‘friend’, or ‘vision of beauty’.
The biblical bearer was a Moabite woman whose loyalty to her mother-in-law is one of Scripture’s most celebrated themes.
Her words ‘Where you go I will go’ are among the most quoted in the Bible.
She gleaned grain in the fields of Boaz, whom she later married.
Through her marriage to Boaz, she became the great-grandmother of King David.
This Hebrew name possibly means ‘companion’ or ‘friend’ — Ruth, the Moabite heroine.
origin: Hebrew, possibly meaning ‘companion’, ‘friend’, or ‘vision of beauty’. Ruth the Moabite is the heroine of the Book of Ruth, famous for her loyalty to Naomi and her words ‘Where you go I will go.’ She married Boaz and became the great-grandmother of King David.

clues: This name derives from the Latin name for an ancient city near Rome, famous for its laurel trees.
A magnificent ruler of Florence during the Renaissance bore this name and was a great patron of the arts.
The root ‘laurus’ (laurel) connects this name to the crown given to victors and poets in antiquity.
He was known as ‘il Magnifico’ and supported artists including Michelangelo and Botticelli.
The laurel wreath from which this name derives was sacred to Apollo, the god of poetry and music.
This name means ‘from Laurentum’ (city of laurels) — Lorenzo, the Italian form of Lawrence.
origin: Italian form of Latin Laurentius, meaning ‘from Laurentum’, a city whose name is related to laurus (‘laurel’). Most famously borne by Lorenzo de’ Medici ‘il Magnifico’ (1449–1492), the Renaissance ruler of Florence and patron of the arts.

clues: This name is the Spanish and Portuguese form of a Greek name associated with purity and sanctity.
The root may be the Greek ‘hagnos’, meaning ‘chaste’ or ‘pure’.
A 14th-century Portuguese noblewoman murdered for her love affair with a prince bore this name.
Her story of forbidden love and posthumous coronation is one of Portugal’s most tragic legends.
After her murder, the prince reportedly exhumed her body and forced the court to kiss her hand as queen.
This is the Spanish/Portuguese form of Agnes — Inés, from Greek ‘hagnos’ (pure).
origin: Spanish/Portuguese form of Agnes, from Greek hagnos (‘chaste/pure’). The most famous bearer is Inês de Castro (c. 1325–1355), a Galician noblewoman whose love affair with Portuguese Prince Pedro led to her murder and his legendary act of posthumously crowning her queen.

clues: This name may derive from a Latin or Greek word relating to a region in southern Italy or to light.
The most famous bearer was a physician and author of both a Gospel and the Acts of the Apostles.
Some scholars link it to the Latin ‘lux’ (light) or to the region of Lucania in Italy.
He is the patron saint of artists, physicians, and surgeons.
A more modern famous bearer is the hero of a space-opera film saga that begins with ‘A New Hope’.
This name possibly means ‘from Lucania’ or ‘light-giving’ — Luke, as in the evangelist.
origin: Possibly from Greek Loukas, meaning ‘from Lucania’ (a region in southern Italy), or related to Latin lux (‘light’). Saint Luke the Evangelist authored the third Gospel and Acts. He is the patron saint of doctors, artists, and surgeons.

clues: This Arabic name is a single word describing one of the most fundamental phenomena in physics.
In Islamic theology, one of the 99 names of God (al-Asma al-Husna) uses this word.
Surah 24 of the Quran is named after this concept and contains the famous ‘Verse of Light’.
The word can refer to both physical illumination and spiritual enlightenment.
A Jordanian queen born Lisa Halaby adopted this name upon her marriage to King Hussein.
This Arabic name means ‘light’ — Noor, as in ‘An-Noor’ (The Light), a name of God in Islam.
origin: Arabic, meaning ‘light’. An-Noor (The Light) is one of the 99 names of God in Islam, and Surah An-Noor (Quran 24) contains the celebrated Verse of Light. Borne by Queen Noor of Jordan (b. 1951). The name is used for both males and females across the Muslim world.

clues: This name derives from a Greek adjective meaning ‘bright’, ‘radiant’, or ‘shining’.
In Greek mythology, the bearer of this name was a Titan goddess associated with the moon.
The same word also became a poetic name for the moon itself in classical literature.
She was the grandmother of Apollo and Artemis through her daughter Leto.
In the New Testament, a deaconess in the early church at Cenchreae also bore this name.
This Greek name means ‘bright’ or ‘radiant’ — Phoebe, from Φοίβη (Phoíbē).
origin: Greek, from Φοίβη (Phoíbē), the feminine of Phoibos (‘bright/radiant’). In mythology, Phoebe was a Titan associated with the moon and the grandmother of Apollo and Artemis. In the New Testament, Phoebe was a deaconess commended by Paul in Romans 16.

clues: This Arabic name has astronomical associations — it originally described something that arrives or appears at night.
An 8th-century Muslim general who crossed into Europe at a famous strait gave his name to a landmark.
The root ‘T-R-Q’ relates to knocking, arriving, or coming by night.
The rock where he landed in 711 CE bears his name to this day — Jabal _____.
The strait between Africa and Europe’s southernmost tip is named for this general’s mountain.
This Arabic name means ‘night visitor’ or ‘morning star’ — Tariq, as in Gibraltar (Jabal Tariq).
origin: Arabic, from the root T-R-Q meaning ‘to knock’ or ‘to arrive at night’. Often translated as ‘night visitor’ or ‘morning star’. Surah At-Tariq (Quran 86) uses this word. Tariq ibn Ziyad (d. 720) was the Berber general whose landing gave Gibraltar (Jabal Tariq, ‘Mountain of Tariq’) its name.

clues: This name derives from one of the most powerful and ancient family names in Roman history.
The gens (clan) associated with this name claimed descent from the goddess Venus herself.
The masculine form of this family name was borne by Rome’s most famous dictator.
The calendar used in the West for centuries takes its name from this family.
The most famous member of the gens was assassinated on the Ides of March, 44 BC.
This is the feminine form of the Roman family name Julius — Julia, possibly meaning ‘youthful’.
origin: Latin, the feminine form of Julius, the name of the gens Julia, one of Rome’s most ancient patrician families. They claimed descent from Iulus, grandson of Venus. The name may derive from Greek ioulos (‘downy-bearded’), suggesting youthfulness. Made immortal by the Julian dynasty, especially Julius Caesar.

clues: This name combines two kanji elements typically relating to robustness and order or governance.
Common kanji pairings include 健 (‘healthy/strong’) + 二 (‘two/second’) or 賢 (‘wise’) + 司 (‘administrate’).
It was particularly popular for second-born sons in traditional Japanese naming conventions.
The ‘ji’ element often indicated birth order in classical Japanese names.
A celebrated Japanese author and poet, _____ Miyazawa, known for ‘Night on the Galactic Railroad’, bore this name.
This Japanese name is Kenji — commonly meaning ‘healthy second son’ or ‘wise ruler’.
origin: Japanese, with meaning dependent on kanji. Common combinations include 健二 (‘healthy second son’), 賢治 (‘wise governance’), and 健司 (‘strong administrator’). Associated with Kenji Miyazawa (1896–1933), the beloved Japanese poet and children’s author.

clues: This name comes from a Latin word that is perhaps the most fundamental verb of existence.
The root ‘vivus’ gives English words like ‘vivid’, ‘vivacious’, and ‘revive’.
In Arthurian legend, an enchantress by this name trapped Merlin in a tower — or a tree, or a cave.
The Latin root simply means ‘alive’ or ‘living’.
A famous British fashion designer, pioneer of punk couture, bore this name — _____ Westwood.
This Latin name means ‘alive’ or ‘living’ — Vivienne, from ‘vivus’.
origin: Latin, from vivus meaning ‘alive’ or ‘living’. In Arthurian legend, Viviane (Lady of the Lake) enchanted Merlin. The name gained fashion-world fame through Vivienne Westwood (1941–2022), the British designer who pioneered punk fashion.

clues: This name derives from an Arabic root relating to beauty, goodness, and excellence.
The most famous bearer was the grandson of the Prophet Muhammad and the second Shia imam.
The root ‘H-S-N’ produces words meaning ‘beautiful’, ‘good’, and ‘handsome’ in Arabic.
He is revered for choosing peace over war, abdicating the caliphate to avoid bloodshed.
His brother Husayn’s name shares the same root but in diminutive form.
This Arabic name means ‘handsome’ or ‘good’ — Hassan, from the root H-S-N.
origin: Arabic, from the root H-S-N meaning ‘to be beautiful/good’. Means ‘handsome’ or ‘good’. Borne by Hasan ibn Ali (624–670), grandson of the Prophet Muhammad and second Shia imam, revered for his piety and his decision to abdicate the caliphate in favour of peace.

clues: This name’s two Greek components relate to ancestry and renown.
The most famous bearer was the last active ruler of an ancient North African kingdom.
The first element ‘kleos’ means ‘glory’ or ‘fame’ in Greek.
She had famous relationships with both Julius Caesar and Mark Antony.
Her death by snakebite (according to legend) marked the end of the Ptolemaic dynasty in Egypt.
This Greek name means ‘glory of the father’ — from ‘kleos’ (glory) + ‘pater’ (father) — Cleopatra.
origin: Greek, from Κλεοπάτρα (Kleopatrā). Combines kleos (‘glory/fame’) and patēr (‘father’), meaning ‘glory of the father’. Most famously borne by Cleopatra VII (69–30 BC), the last pharaoh of Ptolemaic Egypt, known for her intelligence, political acumen, and relationships with Caesar and Antony.

clues: This Sanskrit name relates to brightness, whiteness, and clarity.
The most famous bearer is the central hero of one of the two great epics of ancient India.
On the battlefield, a divine charioteer delivered a famous philosophical discourse to him.
That discourse became one of the most sacred texts in Hinduism.
The Bhagavad Gita is essentially a conversation between him and Lord Krishna.
This Sanskrit name means ‘bright’ or ‘shining’ — Arjun, the hero of the Mahabharata.
origin: Sanskrit, meaning ‘bright’, ‘shining’, or ‘white’. Arjuna is the central hero of the Mahabharata, one of the two great Sanskrit epics. The Bhagavad Gita, one of Hinduism’s holiest texts, is a dialogue between Arjuna and Lord Krishna on the battlefield of Kurukshetra.

clues: This name belongs to a Greek goddess who served as a divine messenger between heaven and earth.
She travelled along a multicoloured arc that spans the sky after rain.
The same word gives its name to the coloured part of the human eye.
A genus of flowering plants, known for their striking purple, blue, and yellow blooms, also bears this name.
She personified the rainbow and carried messages from the gods on Mount Olympus.
This Greek name means ‘rainbow’ — Iris, the messenger goddess who bridged heaven and earth.
origin: Greek, meaning ‘rainbow’. Iris was the goddess of the rainbow and messenger of the gods in Greek mythology. The word also gives its name to the coloured part of the eye and to the iris flower genus. The name has been popular since antiquity.

clues: This Slavic name shares its Greek root with another common European name meaning ‘victory of the people’.
The last tsar of Russia, whose family’s execution ended a 300-year dynasty, bore this name.
The Greek components are ‘nikē’ (victory) and ‘laos’ (people).
A Russian novelist who wrote ‘Dead Souls’ and ‘The Government Inspector’ also bore this name — _____ Gogol.
The last Romanov tsar was _____ II, overthrown in the 1917 revolution.
This is the Russian form of Nicholas — Nikolai, meaning ‘victory of the people’.
origin: Russian form of Greek Nikolaos, from nikē (‘victory’) and laos (‘people’). Borne by Tsar Nikolai II (1868–1918), the last Russian emperor, and by writer Nikolai Gogol (1809–1852). One of the most enduring names in Russian culture.

clues: This name comes from a short Germanic element meaning ‘noble’.
A daughter of William the Conqueror who became Countess of Blois bore this name.
The root appears in dozens of other names including Albert, Adalbert, and many more.
Through her son Stephen, she was the grandmother of an English king.
The Germanic root ‘adal’ is one of the most productive naming elements in medieval European names.
This Germanic name means ‘noble’ — Adela, from ‘adal’ (noble/nobility).
origin: Germanic, from adal meaning ‘noble’. Borne by Adela of Normandy (c. 1067–1137), daughter of William the Conqueror and mother of King Stephen of England. The root adal is one of the most common elements in Germanic names, also appearing in Adelaide, Albert, and Adeline.

clues: This name derives from a Hebrew word associated with peace, wholeness, and completeness.
The most famous bearer was an Israelite king renowned throughout the ancient world for his extraordinary intellect.
The root ‘shalom’ is one of the most recognised Hebrew words globally.
He built the First Temple in Jerusalem and is credited with authoring Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and the Song of Songs.
His judgment in the case of two women claiming the same baby is a legendary example of wisdom.
This Hebrew name means ‘peaceful’ or ‘man of peace’ — Solomon, from ‘shalom’ (peace).
origin: Hebrew, from Shelomoh, related to shalom (‘peace’). King Solomon (c. 970–931 BC) was renowned for his wisdom, wealth, and the building of the First Temple in Jerusalem. He is traditionally credited with authoring several books of the Bible.

clues: This name exists in multiple languages with strikingly different meanings — from illusion to water to love.
In Hindu philosophy, it describes the cosmic force that creates the appearance of the material world.
In Sanskrit, the concept refers to the illusory nature of reality — that the world is not what it seems.
In Greek, it means ‘good mother’; in Hebrew, it relates to water.
A celebrated American poet and civil rights activist bore this name — _____ Angelou.
This multicultural name is Maya — meaning ‘illusion’ in Sanskrit, ‘water’ in Hebrew, ‘good mother’ in Greek.
origin: Multiple origins. In Sanskrit, māyā means ‘illusion’ — a central concept in Hindu and Buddhist philosophy. In Hebrew, it may derive from mayim (‘water’). In Greek, from Maia (‘good mother’), a Pleiad. Famously borne by Maya Angelou (1928–2014), the American poet and memoirist.

clues: This name may derive from a Celtic word meaning ‘noise’ or ‘tumult’, or from the French word for ‘sad’.
The most famous bearer is the hero of one of the great tragic love stories of medieval literature.
The French influence led to an association with ‘triste’ (sad), fitting the story’s tragic ending.
He drank a love potion by accident, binding him fatally to a woman betrothed to his uncle, King Mark.
His doomed love affair with Iseult (or Isolde) inspired Wagner’s opera and countless retellings.
This name belongs to the tragic lover of Iseult — Tristan, possibly from Celtic ‘drest’ (tumult) or French ‘triste’ (sad).
origin: Of debated origin — possibly from Pictish Drest (‘tumult’) or influenced by French triste (‘sad’). Tristan is the hero of the medieval legend of Tristan and Iseult, one of the great tragic love stories of European literature, later immortalised in Wagner’s opera ‘Tristan und Isolde’.

clues: This name has independent roots in both South Asian and Northern European languages.
In Sanskrit, it means ‘grace’ or ‘brilliance’; in Scandinavian languages, it’s a diminutive of a longer name.
The Scandinavian origin connects it to the Hebrew name Hannah, meaning ‘grace’.
Remarkably, both the Sanskrit and Scandinavian meanings converge on the same concept of grace.
The Scandinavian form is a pet form of Anna/Annika, itself from Hebrew.
This name means ‘grace’ in both Sanskrit and Scandinavian — Anika.
origin: Dual origins: from Sanskrit meaning ‘grace’, ‘brilliance’, or ‘splendour’, and independently from Scandinavian as a diminutive of Anna (from Hebrew Hannah, also meaning ‘grace’). The convergence of meanings across unrelated languages has contributed to its global popularity.

clues: This Hebrew name describes a divine figure whose role involves extraordinary strength or power.
In the Abrahamic traditions, the bearer is one of the archangels — a messenger of the highest order.
The first element ‘gever’ means ‘strong man’ or ‘hero’ in Hebrew.
In Islam, this archangel (Jibril) is believed to have transmitted the Quran to the Prophet Muhammad.
He announced the births of both John the Baptist and Jesus in the New Testament.
This Hebrew name means ‘God is my strength’ — from ‘gever’ (strong man) + ‘El’ (God) — Gabriel.
origin: Hebrew, combining gever (‘strong man/hero’) and El (‘God’), meaning ‘God is my strength’. Gabriel is one of the archangels in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. In Islam, Jibril transmitted the Quran to the Prophet Muhammad. In Christianity, he announced the birth of Jesus to Mary.

clues: This name is the feminine form of a Greek word that describes a solid, immovable natural feature.
An ancient Nabataean city carved into rose-coloured cliffs in modern Jordan shares this name.
The masculine form was given by Jesus to one of his disciples as a symbolic name.
The disciple was told ‘upon this _____ I will build my church’ in the Gospel of Matthew.
The UNESCO World Heritage Site in Jordan, carved from sandstone, is one of the New Seven Wonders.
This Greek name means ‘rock’ or ‘stone’ — Petra, the feminine of Petros (Peter).
origin: Greek, meaning ‘rock’ or ‘stone’, the feminine form of Petros (Peter). Also the name of the ancient Nabataean city in Jordan, carved from rose-red sandstone and designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The name Peter was given by Jesus to the apostle Simon as a symbol of steadfastness.

clues: This name is a single Latin word for a specific large, powerful animal.
Thirteen popes have chosen this name, more than almost any other papal name.
The animal it describes is associated with courage, royalty, and strength across cultures.
It is also the name of a zodiac constellation visible in the spring sky.
The animal is known as the ‘king of the jungle’ despite not actually living in jungles.
This Latin name means ‘lion’ — Leo.
origin: Latin, meaning ‘lion’. Thirteen popes have taken this name, including Leo the Great (c. 400–461) who persuaded Attila the Hun to withdraw from Italy. Also the name of a zodiac sign and constellation. The name has surged in popularity across Europe and the Americas in recent years.

clues: This name derives from an Arabic root that entered Swahili and describes a core spiritual concept.
In the African American holiday of Kwanzaa, this word represents the seventh and final principle.
The Arabic root ‘A-M-N’ relates to trust, belief, and security.
The Kwanzaa principle it represents is considered the foundation that gives meaning to all struggle and effort.
It is the last of the seven principles (Nguzo Saba) and was chosen to represent spiritual conviction.
This Swahili/Arabic name means ‘faith’ or ‘belief’ — Imani.
origin: Swahili, from Arabic īmān meaning ‘faith’ or ‘belief’, from the root A-M-N (‘to be faithful/secure’). Imani is the seventh principle of Kwanzaa, representing faith. The name is popular across East Africa and the African diaspora.

clues: This name is a Renaissance-era compound of two Latin names, both relating to greatness.
A Habsburg Holy Roman Emperor in the 15th century is credited with popularising this combined form.
The first component comes from ‘Maximus’ (greatest); the second from ‘Aemilianus’.
A tragic 19th-century emperor of Mexico, installed by Napoleon III and executed by firing squad, bore this name.
The name was allegedly created by Emperor Frederick III for his son, fusing two Roman heroes’ names.
This name combines Maximus + Aemilianus into Maximilian, meaning ‘greatest Aemilianus’.
origin: A Latin compound, traditionally said to combine Maximus (‘greatest’) and Aemilianus (from the Roman gens Aemilia). Popularised by Holy Roman Emperor Maximilian I (1459–1519). Also borne by Emperor Maximilian I of Mexico (1832–1867), whose execution was painted by Manet.

clues: This Arabic name describes the period of darkness between sunset and sunrise.
A tragic love story from 7th-century Arabia made this name synonymous with unattainable love.
The Arabic root ‘L-Y-L’ relates to the nocturnal hours.
A famous rock guitar anthem by Eric Clapton bears this name, inspired by the same ancient love story.
The lover driven mad by his devotion to her was called Majnun (‘madman’).
This Arabic name means ‘night’ — Layla, the beloved of Majnun.
origin: Arabic, from layl meaning ‘night’. The story of Layla and Majnun, originating in 7th-century Arabia, is one of the great tragic love stories of the Islamic world. Eric Clapton’s 1970 song ‘Layla’ was inspired by this tale after he read Nizami’s version.

clues: This name derives from a medieval Italian city famous for its distinctive reddish-brown earth pigment.
The city in Tuscany is known for its shell-shaped piazza and a dramatic bareback horse race.
The colour ‘burnt _____’ and ‘raw _____’ both refer to pigments named after this city.
The Palio, a bareback horse race around the city’s main square, has been held since the 17th century.
Artists have used the warm brown pigment named after this city for centuries.
This name comes from the Italian city of Siena — Sienna, associated with the warm earth-tone colour.
origin: From Siena, a city in Tuscany, Italy. The name entered use as a given name through the pigment ‘sienna’ (raw and burnt), a warm reddish-brown earth colour used by artists since the Renaissance. The city itself may take its name from the Roman Saena Julia. Popularised as a baby name in the late 20th century.
